questions.question: What is the core idea behind Dependency Injection?
options: A class receives the objects it needs from outside rather than creating them itself
A class extends a base class to inherit functionality
A class uses static methods to avoid instantiation
A class caches its dependencies to avoid repeated creation

questions.question: Why is field injection with @Autowired considered problematic in Spring?
options: It causes circular dependencies automatically
It makes the class impossible to instantiate without a full Spring context, breaking unit tests
It is slower than constructor injection at runtime
It only works with @Service beans, not @Repository or @Controller

questions.question: In Angular, which decorator registers a class with the DI container so it can be injected elsewhere?
options: @Component
@Module
@Injectable
@Inject

questions.question: What is the main testing benefit of constructor injection?
options: Tests run faster because constructors are compiled to native code
You can pass mock implementations directly without a framework or container
The compiler automatically generates test doubles for injected types
Unit tests automatically spin up a Spring context to resolve dependencies

questions.question: In Spring Boot, if a class has exactly one constructor and no @Autowired annotation, what does Spring do?
options: Throws a NoSuchBeanDefinitionException
Creates the bean with null dependencies
Automatically injects dependencies through that single constructor
Requires you to add @Autowired explicitly to the constructor
